title: An Improved Line-Point Low-Degree Test
speaker: Prahladh Harsha
abstract: In this talk, I'll show that the most natural low-degree test for polynomials over finite fields is ``robust'' in the high-error regime for linear-sized fields. This settles a long-standing open question in the area of low-degree testing, yielding an O(d)-query robust test in the ``high-error'' regime. The previous results in this space either worked only in the "low-error" regime (Polishchuk & Spielman, STOC 1994), or required q=Ω(d4)

proof_stages.label: Low-degree testing problem setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a finite field F_q, degree d<q, and ambient dimension m, and formulates the sublinear-query problem of deciding whether a table f:F_q^m→F_q is the evaluation of a total-degree-d polynomial or is far from every such polynomial.

proof_stages.label: Classical line-point test and low-error stability
proof_stages.summary: The classical Rubinfeld-Sudan line-point test is introduced, its completeness is immediate, and prior low-error soundness/stability results are reviewed, including their role in PCP constructions and the improvement to q linear in d.

proof_stages.label: High-error regime and previous work
proof_stages.summary: The speaker shifts to the regime where the line test accepts with small but non-random probability, reviews the Arora-Sudan line-test theorem with q polynomially large in d and the Raz-Safra plane-test theorem with q linear in d, and surveys later derandomized, small-field, and higher-dimensional variants.

proof_stages.label: Main line-point theorem and methodological gap
proof_stages.summary: The main result is stated as the missing common strengthening: the high-error soundness theorem for the original line-point test with q=Ω(d), requiring a return to polynomial-method arguments because combinatorial plane-intersection methods do not apply to intersecting lines.

proof_stages.label: Polishchuk-Spielman divisibility lemma
proof_stages.summary: As the algebraic backbone of the earlier low-error analysis, the Polishchuk-Spielman lemma is recast as a local-to-global divisibility statement for a trivariate polynomial linear in z whose restrictions to many rows and columns have roots of the form z-c(t).

proof_stages.label: High-degree Polishchuk-Spielman generalization
proof_stages.summary: The speaker formulates a new higher-z-degree analogue: if a polynomial A(x,y,z) has such roots on sufficiently many lines through sufficiently many centers and directions, then it has a global factor/root z-Q(x,y), and explains how this algebraic statement supplies the hidden low-degree polynomial in the line-test analysis.

proof_stages.label: Proof via good centers, Hensel lifting, and Schwartz-Zippel
proof_stages.summary: The proof sketch selects a center where A(α,β,z) has no repeated roots using a discriminant argument, moves it to the origin, uniquely lifts the chosen root by univariate and bivariate Newton/Hensel iteration, and upgrades the resulting modular identity to an exact identity using its agreement on many lines and a Schwartz-Zippel argument.

proof_stages.label: Conclusion and relation to PCP applications
proof_stages.summary: The talk concludes that the high-degree generalization yields the desired line-point soundness theorem and unifies the earlier low-error line-test and high-error plane-test results, while noting that no new PCP application is presently claimed because derandomized plane-test analyses already exist.

examples.name: GF(2) small-field modification
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker explains that over GF(2) a line contains too few points to test degree d, so one instead reads a d+1-dimensional subspace; this illustrates why the talk restricts to large fields.
examples.engagement_reason: he explains why ordinary line tests give no information over GF(2) and describes the concrete modified test using a d+1 dimensional subspace

passes.reason: GF(2) is the smallest finite-field case where line restrictions have too few points, obstructing the unmodified line-point test.

GF(2) is the smallest finite-field boundary case, where lines have too few points and the usual line test collapses, forcing a modified subspace test.
GF(2) is the minimal field where line restrictions collapse to two points, used to show the ordinary line test must be modified.

Over GF(2) a line has only two points, so the usual line test is vacuous and must be replaced by the minimal small-field subspace test.
GF(2) is the smallest finite field, where lines have too few points for the usual line test to be informative, forcing a modified subspace test.
GF(2) is the smallest finite field, and its two-point lines are used to show the ordinary line test gives no information without modification.
GF(2) is the minimal finite field, where lines have only two points and the usual line test loses degree information, forcing a subspace-test modification.

examples.name: Function high-degree in one axis
examples.note: A function that is low-degree in all but one coordinate direction would pass most axis-parallel line checks, illustrating why axis-parallel tests do not control total degree in high dimension.
examples.engagement_reason: he reasons through a function low-degree in all but one coordinate direction and estimates that an axis-parallel test passes with probability about 1-1/m

passes.reason: A function low-degree in all but one coordinate is an ordinary adversarial input showing axis-parallel tests miss a total-degree violation.
A function that is high-degree in only one coordinate is an ordinary anisotropic bad case used to show axis-parallel tests can miss total degree.
A function low-degree in all but one coordinate is an ordinary anisotropic construction used to show axis-parallel tests can miss high total degree.
A function with high degree hidden in one coordinate is used as an adversarial case showing axis-parallel tests can miss total degree.
The function has the minimal one-bad-axis defect maximizing acceptance by an axis-parallel test, so it is used as an enemy case.
A function that is high-degree in exactly one axis is a minimal adversarial case passing most axis-parallel checks while defeating total-degree soundness.
A function high-degree in exactly one axis is a one-bad-direction boundary case that makes the axis-parallel test accept with probability about 1-1/m.
A function with only one high-degree axis is a worst-case obstruction to axis-parallel testing, passing all but a 1/m fraction of directions.
A function bad in only one coordinate direction is a minimal obstruction that passes the axis-parallel test with maximal probability about 1-1/m.
A function with high degree in exactly one coordinate direction is an adversarial boundary case for axis-parallel tests, passing all but about a 1/m fraction of directions.
A function with exactly one high-degree axis is the minimal bad direction case that makes the axis-parallel test accept with high probability despite failure of total low degree.
A function bad in only one axis is a minimally bad adversarial case showing axis-parallel tests can miss total-degree failure.

examples.name: Single-direction divisibility counterexample
examples.note: The polynomial xz-cy has a univariate root after fixing most x-values but no global bivariate root z-q(x,y), showing why the Polishchuk-Spielman local-to-global lemma needs more than one direction.
examples.engagement_reason: he gives the one-direction divisibility counterexample xz-cy and checks that restrictions in one direction have a root while no global bivariate root exists

passes.reason: The polynomial such as xz-cy is used to show one direction of divisibility checks can have local roots without a global polynomial root.

The polynomial such as xz-cy shows that checking divisibility along only one family of lines does not force a global polynomial root.

The ordinary polynomial xz-cy is used to show that checking divisibility in only one direction does not force a global polynomial root.
The polynomial xz-cy has local polynomial roots on almost every specialization in one direction but no global polynomial root, showing one direction is not enough.
The polynomial like xz-cy is a minimal one-direction obstruction to inferring global divisibility from local restrictions.
The linear polynomial xz-cy is a minimal one-direction obstruction with local divisibility but no global polynomial root.

examples.name: Quadratic two-direction counterexample
examples.note: The product (xz-cy)(yz-rx) has appropriate roots on the two coordinate-direction families but no global root z-q(x,y), motivating the need for more direction families when the z-degree is larger than one.
examples.engagement_reason: he constructs the quadratic-in-z example (xz-cy)(yz-rx) and verifies it fools two directions but has no global root z-q(x,y)

examples.name: Hilbert irreducibility analogy
examples.note: The speaker compares the desired specialization-to-global factorization statement with Hilbert irreducibility, while emphasizing that the proof needs much tighter finite-field parameter control.
examples.engagement_reason: Hilbert irreducibility is invoked only as an analogy to factorization under restriction, without carrying out an argument on it
